title: Annotator Agreement Challenges
concept: Evaluator bias and vague guidelines lead to disagreement; tracking inter-annotator agreement is essential to build clean preference sets.

exercises.prompt: Why do human evaluators sometimes disagree on whether an AI response is good or bad?

options.text: They are always distracted by background noise.
They have different personal biases and vague guidelines.
They are trying to trick the AI developers.

exercises.explain: Human evaluators often disagree because they interpret guidelines differently or have personal biases about what makes an answer 'good.'

cards.title: Why Can't We Agree?
cards.body: Have you ever argued with a friend over which movie is 'better'? One of you might love action, while the other prefers comedy. Because you have different personal preferences, you disagree. The same thing happens to human trainers grading AI models!

cards.title: Inter-Annotator Agreement
cards.body: Inter-Annotator Agreement is a score that measures how often different human evaluators agree on the same data points. If the agreement score is high, it means your instructions are clear. If it is low, your guidelines are likely too vague, or your evaluators are biased!

cards.title: The Restaurant Critics
cards.body: Imagine asking three food critics to rate a restaurant. If you just ask 'Is the food good?', one might say 'Yes' because it is spicy, another 'No' because it is too salty, and the third 'Maybe' because they only liked the dessert. Without a clear scorecard, their reviews will be a chaotic mess!

cards.title: Clear vs. Vague Guidelines
cards.body: Say we want to label AI responses as 'polite' or 'rude.' If our guidelines say 'Label as rude if the bot sounds cold,' one evaluator might label 'I cannot do that' as rude, while another labels it as polite. By changing the guidelines to 'Label as rude only if it uses offensive language,' the evaluators will agree much more often.

cards.title: Myth: Humans Naturally Agree
cards.body: You might think that human evaluators will naturally agree on what a 'good' AI response looks like. In reality, without strict, objective rules, human bias and different interpretations lead to wild disagreements. Even expert labelers need clear, written guidelines!

cards.title: How to Guide Evaluators
cards.body: See how changing the way we ask questions improves the consistency of our human trainers' evaluations.

left.text: 'Is this AI response good?' (Leads to high disagreement because 'good' is personal)

right.text: 'Does the AI response answer the user's question without spelling errors?' (Leads to high agreement)

exercises.prompt: What does a high inter-annotator agreement score tell you about your AI feedback dataset?

options.text: The evaluators are grading too quickly.
The guidelines are clear, and the data is consistent.
The AI model is generating perfect answers.
The evaluators are using different sets of rules.

exercises.explain: A high inter-annotator agreement score indicates that different evaluators consistently give the same ratings, which means your dataset is reliable and consistent.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Personal bias can cause human evaluators to grade the exact same AI response differently.
exercises.explain: Personal bias is a major reason why annotators disagree, which is why we need objective rubrics instead of subjective opinions.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of low agreement scores.
exercises.sentence: Inter-annotator agreement is usually very low when the scoring guidelines are too vague.
exercises.explain: When guidelines are too vague or open to interpretation, different evaluators will use their own personal biases, leading to poor agreement scores.

exercises.prompt: Identify the response generated by an AI bot rather than a human guideline or instruction:

options.text: Guideline: 'Rate the response as helpful if it answers the user prompt in under 3 sentences.'
Bot: 'As an AI, I am unable to assist with personal opinions, but I can provide facts.'
Instructions: 'Please compare Response A and Response B and select the one that feels friendlier.'

exercises.explain: Prompt B is the only one that represents an AI response, as it mimics a classic robotic refusal template. Prompts A and C are instructions written by human managers or designers.

exercises.prompt: Your team of human evaluators is constantly arguing over which AI summary is 'more helpful.' Half say Summary A is better because it is short, while the other half say Summary B is better because it is detailed. What is the best way to resolve this issue and get clean preference data?

options.text: Pick the annotator who has worked at the company longest and only use their scores.
Update the guidelines with specific, clear rubrics and examples of what makes an answer good or bad.
Flip a coin whenever there is a tie between the two evaluators.
Delete all the data points where the evaluators disagreed and start over with new data.

exercises.explain: When annotators disagree because the instructions are too vague, the best fix is to make the rules objective and clear. Adding a tie-breaker or choosing one person's opinion doesn't fix the underlying confusion.
